system.biography:
Physical Appearance

Astrid stands four feet eleven inches tall, small-framed and wiry at ninety-two pounds. At twenty-three, she moves with the abrupt, decisive energy of a diving seabird - sudden stillness followed by committed action. Her blonde hair is typically windswept from sea air, and her blue eyes carry the sharp focus of a predator scanning water from height. Norwegian-pale skin shows sun exposure from maritime life, with scattered scars from her careless approach to magic and the occasional botched spell.

Her face is fine-boned but intense, usually set in concentration when working or scanning the horizon when at rest. Small, calloused hands move precisely when casting - she's learned to work without grand gestures, economizing movement. She dresses practically for ship life in layered woolens and leather, favoring blues and grays that blend with sea and sky. Minimal adornment: a silver pendant marking her Bjornaer heritage, occasionally feathers worked into braids.

Her presence combines focus with restlessness. She paces decks, climbs rigging, and seeks high vantage points - uncomfortable with confinement, always needing to see the horizon. When she commits to action, there's no hesitation; when she's uncertain, she moves constantly. Those who know her recognize the pattern: when she goes still and watchful, she's identified her target. When she transforms to gannet form, observers understand why - the bird's focused dive perfectly expresses her approach to problems.

Born: 1182, Norway

House: Bjornaer (Clan Ilfetu)

Heartbeast: Gannet

Former Covenant: Cnoc nam Beathaichean-cridhe (Hill of the Heartbeasts), Scotland

Parens: Morag Craicfhionn

Current Status (1205): Recently joined Solving's sailing covenant after passing her gauntlet

Childhood in Norway (1182-1186)

Astrid was born in Norway during a period of conflict and instability. Her early childhood was marked by the displacement common to that era - her family were refugees fleeing violence, though the specific circumstances remain unclear to her. What she remembers: cold, hunger, movement, fear.

Around age four, her Gift manifested. This marked her as different and dangerous in an already precarious situation. Her family, struggling to survive themselves, recognized they couldn't protect or train a Gifted child. Through channels lost to memory, she was given to someone to be brought to Scotland and eventually to Cnoc nam Beathaichean-cridhe.

The journey itself is fragmented in her memory - ships, cold water, strangers' hands guiding her, the smell of salt and fear. She arrived in Scotland displaced and alone, carrying only her native Norwegian language and the bone-deep knowledge that home can vanish.

Apprenticeship at Cnoc nam Beathaichean-cridhe (1188-1203)

The Covenant

Cnoc nam Beathaichean-cridhe - the Hill of the Heartbeasts - served as the spiritual center of House Bjornaer in the British Isles. Located in the Scottish Highlands, it specialized in the mysteries of transformation and maintained strong ties to Clan Ilfetu, the divinatory tradition within the House. The covenant emphasized understanding one's heartbeast as a path to self-knowledge, and practiced the old rituals in Gothic, the House's dead ceremonial language.

Morag Craicfhionn

Astrid's parens was a senior Bjornaer with a seal heartbeast, known for producing capable students who left her tutelage well-prepared but emotionally self-sufficient. Morag's approach was unsentimental but deeply caring - she gave Astrid what she needed to succeed, then prepared her to leave.

Their relationship resembled mother and child more than the typical master-apprentice dynamic, though Morag never indulged sentiment. She taught Astrid that caring meant equipping people to survive independently, not creating dependence. When Astrid succeeded, Morag showed satisfaction through increased expectations. When Astrid failed, Morag ensured she understood the lesson, then moved forward without dwelling on it.

Morag recognized early that Astrid would need to leave Scotland - her restlessness, her connection to the sea, her drive to master the mysteries all pointed toward travel. Rather than trying to keep her close, Morag deliberately prepared her for departure. When Astrid passed her gauntlet in 1203, Morag's pride was evident in how readily she let her go.

Training and Development

Astrid's fifteen-year apprenticeship divided into two distinct phases, reflecting House Bjornaer's tradition of separating mystical initiation from practical Hermetic education.

Early Training with Morag (1188-1200)

From Morag: Core Hermetic theory, Arts (particularly Muto and Animal), the heartbeast transformation, and foundational magical practice. Morag emphasized precision over power, understanding over force. She taught Astrid to see magic as expression of natural principles rather than imposed will - the gannet doesn't force fish to appear, it learns to see where they are.

During these twelve years, Morag built Astrid's foundation methodically: Latin literacy, laboratory technique, theoretical understanding of the Arts, and the discipline required for consistent magical practice. She recognized early that Astrid's impatient nature required careful channeling - teaching her to calculate risks before committing to action, even though the calculation always felt frustratingly slow.

From Clan Ilfetu: Parallel to Morag's instruction, Astrid received extra training in House rituals, blood divination (haemagomania), Gothic language, and knowledge of the Great Beasts. The fifty extra experience points from Clan Ilfetu membership went to Bjornaer Lore, Magic Lore (Great Beasts specialty), and Gothic. This mystical education ran alongside her practical Hermetic training, showing her that transformation revealed deeper truths than mere physical change.

From covenant culture: The understanding that transformation reveals truth. Your heartbeast shows what you are beneath social conditioning. Astrid learned to read others through their animal forms and to understand herself through the gannet - not just the bird's physical capabilities, but what choosing that form revealed about her essential nature.

The Transition (1200)

In 1200, at age 18, Morag initiated Astrid into the Outer Mystery of the Heartbeast. She became Bjornaer, capable of transformation, carrying the House's fundamental capability. This marked the end of Morag's primary teaching responsibility but not the end of her apprenticeship.

Morag's focus shifted to teaching Astrid about her role as future Mystagogue - one who would eventually initiate others into the mysteries. She taught the rituals, the philosophy, the responsibility of passing knowledge to the next generation. These lessons continued throughout Astrid's final three years but represented guidance rather than formal instruction.

Final Training with Uthric (1200-1203)

Morag transferred primary teaching responsibility to Uthric Bloðulfrinn of Clan Midusulf, a practical-minded maga whose wolf heartbeast represented a different approach to House Bjornaer traditions. This was deliberate strategy: Astrid needed what Morag couldn't effectively provide.

From Uthric: Hermetic law and political reality, combat magic and tactical thinking, practical survival skills, and the broader context of the Order beyond House Bjornaer. Where Morag taught precision and understanding, Uthric taught application and survival. He emphasized reading hostile intentions, knowing when to fight versus negotiate, and understanding how other Houses actually behaved rather than how they claimed to behave.

Uthric assumed Astrid's competence from day one, throwing multiple challenges simultaneously and expecting her to prioritize and adapt. He taught her Mastering the Unruly Beast (control animals in combat), Jupiter's Resounding Blow (disable opponents), and the tactical use of her gannet form for reconnaissance and strike attacks. His lessons were blunt: "Flambeau will test you. Tremere will try to recruit or neutralize you. Bonisagus think they own knowledge. Know what you're walking into."

The contrast between teachers proved valuable. Morag had shaped her to understand herself; Uthric equipped her to protect that self. Morag taught mystical depth; Uthric taught worldly breadth. Together they produced a maga who could pursue mysteries without becoming impractical, who understood transformation's spiritual significance without losing sight of its tactical applications.

From personal study: Throughout both phases, Astrid developed deep knowledge of the North Sea environment, becoming expert in its weather patterns, creatures, and magical phenomena. Her Ways of the Sea virtue reflects this thorough understanding - partly taught by both instructors, partly discovered through her own observation and experience. She spent countless hours in gannet form, learning to read wind and wave, understanding how weather systems moved, recognizing the signs that preceded storms.

The Gannet Heartbeast

Astrid's heartbeast revealed itself during her apprenticeship as the gannet - a plunge-diving seabird known for spectacular hunting dives from height. The choice was revelatory: Astrid approaches problems like a gannet hunts fish. She observes from a distance, identifies her target, commits completely to the dive, and accepts the risk of impact.

This manifests in her personality:

  • Focus: Once she's committed, she doesn't waver
  • Impatience: The time between identifying the target and diving feels wasteful
  • Directness: No feinting or misdirection, just the straight dive
  • Risk acceptance: Gannets can injure themselves diving; she accepts that success requires vulnerability

The gannet also shaped her magical development. She learned Quiet and Subtle Magic specifically to cast while in bird form, reducing the normal -15 penalty to just -5. This allows her to use magic while scouting or traveling in heartbeast form.

Bras the Capricorn

During her apprenticeship, Astrid encountered Bras - a magical capricorn (goat-fish hybrid) being pursued by predators in Scottish waters. Without hesitation, she dove into the cold water to interpose herself between Bras and danger.

The rescue succeeded, but it bonded them. Bras, intelligent enough to understand he owed his life to this small maga, chose to stay with her. She accepted responsibility for his wellbeing, understanding that her impulsive dive had created an obligation.

Their relationship is protective but not codependent. Astrid ensures Bras can survive and thrive independently; Bras provides companionship and occasionally assistance. She learned from Morag that true care means preparing others to stand alone, and she applies this to Bras. He's her companion, not her pet.

Study Requirement and Adaptations

Astrid cannot study magic from books or vis alone - she requires the presence of natural phenomena corresponding to the Art she's studying. To study Aquam, she needs running water or ocean. To study Muto, she needs living things undergoing transformation. To study Animal, she needs creatures present.

This major limitation shaped her entire approach:

  • She studies outdoors almost exclusively
  • She times her study to seasonal and weather phenomena
  • She travels to access the environments she needs
  • She struggles with Terram (requires earth/stone) more than other Arts
  • Laboratory work requires careful staging of appropriate conditions

Combined with her Environmental Sensitivity to enclosed spaces, this means traditional covenant life is nearly impossible for her. She needs open air, natural phenomena, and freedom of movement. The sailing covenant is ideal - the ship provides mobile access to her primary study environments.

Careless Sorcerer and Magic

Astrid casts with commitment rather than caution, accepting botch risk as the price of decisive action. She rolls two extra botch dice when casting spells - her gannet approach applied to spellcasting. When she commits to the spell, she commits fully, and sometimes impacts hard.

This combines dangerously with her tendency toward action. She's the maga most likely to cast a spell immediately when others are still planning. Sometimes this wins through speed; sometimes it goes catastrophically wrong. Morag taught her to calculate risks before the dive, but Astrid's impatience often overrides calculation.

Her Quiet and Subtle Magic virtues allow her to cast effectively in heartbeast form, reducing the normal -15 penalty to just -5. This makes her dangerous in gannet form - she can scout, then attack from an unexpected angle without needing to transform back.

Visions

Astrid experiences spontaneous visions of emotionally or magically charged events - past, future, or distant occurrences, often symbolic or confusing. They come at quiet moments or in places of power.

She doesn't seek these visions and doesn't fully understand them. They're simply something that happens to her, like the weather. She reports them when relevant but doesn't build her life around them. Unlike Solving's vision-driven quest, her visions are passive experiences rather than driving motivations.

The visions tend to nudge her when it’s time to move on, to give her a sense of what to anticipate and prepare for.

Clan Ilfetu and the Mysteries (1200-1205)

Initiation

During her later apprenticeship, Astrid was formally initiated into Clan Ilfetu, the divinatory tradition within House Bjornaer. This brought:

  • Fifty additional experience points for House-specific knowledge
  • Training in haemagomania (divination through animal blood patterns)
  • Deeper understanding of the Great Beasts (legendary magical animals)
  • Responsibility to continue and transmit the mysteries

Clan Ilfetu's approach resonated with Astrid's nature - finding truth through observation of blood and bone, reading meaning in natural patterns, understanding that transformation and death reveal hidden realities.

The Drive to Learn

Astrid's Driven flaw manifests as obsessive pursuit of the Ilfetu mysteries. She needs to meet the elders scattered across Europe, learn the deeper initiations, master the techniques that will let her see truth beneath surface appearance.

This isn't academic curiosity - it's fundamental to her identity. As Bjornaer, her heartbeast defines who she is. As Ilfetu, the mysteries will reveal what that means. She's driven to understand herself through understanding the tradition.

Morag recognized this drive and deliberately didn't try to satisfy it. Some knowledge must be sought, not given. She pointed Astrid toward the elders who could teach her, then equipped her to travel. The rest was Astrid's responsibility.

Current Mission

The sailing covenant provides perfect opportunity - travel across the North Sea and beyond, seeking Ilfetu elders wherever they've settled. She knows she'll spend years on this path. The mysteries are deep, the elders are scattered, and true understanding takes time. Her impatience makes this difficult, but her commitment makes it inevitable.

Key NPCs and Relationships

Morag Craicfhionn (Parens): The unsentimental mother-figure who prepared Astrid to leave. Their parting was affectionate but practical - Morag confirmed Astrid was ready, offered final advice, and sent her away. No dramatics, no attempts to prolong contact. Astrid knows she can return if truly necessary, but doing so would mean she'd failed to become independent. She carries Morag's lessons: care means equipping people to succeed alone.

Bras the Capricorn: Intelligent magical companion she rescued impulsively. Size +1, Might 9 (10 - Size). Cunning rather than brilliant, loyal but independent. She ensures he can survive without her; he chooses to stay anyway. Their relationship is protective but healthy - neither is dependent on the other.

Ilfetu Elders: Scattered across Europe, these senior Bjornaer know the deeper mysteries. Astrid seeks them out, learning what each can teach. Some will be generous with knowledge; others will demand proof of worthiness. This quest will define her development as a maga.

Personality and Motivations (1205)

Core Traits

Impatient +3 (Patient -3): Once she's identified her target, waiting feels intolerable. She wants to commit to action immediately. This serves her well when decisive action is needed; it creates problems when careful planning would be wiser. She's learned to calculate risks before the dive, but the time between calculation and action is painful. She paces, fidgets, makes preliminary movements - the gannet circling before the plunge.

Curious +1: Genuinely interested in how things work, what patterns mean, why phenomena occur. This fuels her magical study and her pursuit of the mysteries. She asks questions, investigates unusual occurrences, and seeks to understand rather than simply accept. This combines well with her Intelligence but can lead her into danger when curiosity overcomes caution.

Generous +2: She gives freely what others need to succeed - knowledge, resources, protection, equipment. This is Morag's model: caring means preparing people to stand independently. She'll share what she knows, provide what she can, and protect those who need help. But she won't coddle or create dependence. If you want her help, be prepared to use it to become self-sufficient.

Meeting Solving and Khalida (Late 1204/Early 1205)

The Encounter at the Coastal Regio

Solving and Khalida were investigating a tidal regio along the Scottish coast when they discovered Bras - a capricorn, intelligent and magical, swimming in the shallows. They approached to examine the creature and assess the aura's properties.

Astrid was hunting nearby in gannet form when she sensed the magical aura and flew to investigate. She saw two foreign magi - one Moorish, one oddly dressed - approaching a vulnerable magical creature. Without hesitation, she dove.

The gannet plummeted from height. Astrid materialized between the strangers and Bras, ready to protect Bras; there was a tense standoff.

The Agreement

Tension defused as intentions clarified. They introduced themselves - Solving explaining his vision-driven quest northward, Khalida providing practical context about needing maritime expertise. Astrid asked pointed questions about their plans, resources, and structure.

The answers revealed their situation: no permanent base, traveling by hired transport, planning to acquire a vessel and establish a mobile covenant. They needed someone who knew these waters and could work weather magic.

Astrid recognized the opportunity immediately. A traveling covenant meant continuous access to environments her Study Requirement demanded, flexibility to pursue Ilfetu elders across Europe, and the chance to develop her maritime specialization. They needed exactly what she could provide.

She explained her situation: recently gauntleted, required travel for House responsibilities and magical study, possessed deep North Sea knowledge but needed to develop practical maritime spells. The match was obvious.

They agreed to provisional arrangement: she'd accompany them while they finalized covenant plans.

Departure

Astrid returned to Cnoc nam Beathaichean-cridhe to inform her teachers. Morag recognized Astrid had found what she needed and sent her forward. She gathered her minimal possessions and rejoined Solving and Khalida.

The founding moment shaped their dynamic: Solving values her decisive action, Khalida respects her competence and directness, and Astrid protects what she's committed to - which now includes her fellow magi.

Relationship with Solving

She's pragmatic about his vision. Solving saw something in the North Sea that drove him to create this covenant; fine, that gives her the mobile platform she needs. Whether his vision is prophetic truth or mystical delusion doesn't matter - it's producing the outcome she requires.

Current Motivations (1205)

Master the Ilfetu Mysteries: Her driving obsession. She needs to find the elders, learn the deeper techniques, understand what her heartbeast truly means. This will take years and will shape her entire career.

Protect the Covenant: She's committed to this group now. She'll defend it, contribute her expertise, and help others succeed. This is how she expresses care - providing what the covenant needs to survive.

Understand Her Visions: They come without her seeking them. She'd like to understand what they mean and whether they serve some purpose. But this is secondary to her primary drive.

Ways of the Sea

Astrid's deep understanding of maritime environments provides tangible benefits:

  • +3 to all rolls involving the sea and its inhabitants
  • One fewer botch die on sea-related actions (combined with Careless Sorcerer, she rolls +1 botch die at sea instead of +2 elsewhere)
  • Sea creatures don't normally molest her; even aggressive ones recognize her as belonging there
  • Intuitive understanding of weather, currents, tides, and maritime phenomena

This is more than magical knowledge - it's fundamental attunement. She reads the sea like a native language, understanding meanings others miss. The North Sea is her natural environment; she's more comfortable on deck in a storm than in a quiet library.

Small Frame and Physical Limitations

At 4'11" and 92 pounds with Size -1, Astrid is physically vulnerable in direct confrontation. She takes wounds more easily (four-point increments instead of five), tires more quickly from physical exertion, and lacks the strength for heavy labor.

This reinforces her reliance on magic, transformation, and mobility rather than direct force. As a gannet, she's fast and maneuverable. As a human, she's small but precise. She's learned to work with her physical limitations rather than against them.

Her strength isn't in endurance or force - it's in the committed strike at the right moment. Like the gannet diving, she makes the single decisive action count.

Notes for Gamemaster

Careless Sorcerer in Play: She rolls two extra botch dice normally, one extra die at sea (Ways of the Sea reduces by one). She casts decisively and accepts the risk. Most times it works; occasionally it goes catastrophically wrong. She shrugs off botches and tries again - that's the price of commitment.

Study Requirement: She cannot study magic from books or vis without appropriate natural phenomena present. This requires:

  • Studying outdoors near the relevant phenomena
  • Timing study to seasonal availability
  • Traveling to access needed environments
  • Creative solutions for difficult Arts (Terram requires earth/stone, difficult at sea)

This major limitation shapes her entire lifestyle and makes traditional covenant life impossible. It's why the sailing covenant works - she has constant access to her primary study requirements.

Environmental Sensitivity: Enclosed spaces impose -3 to Stamina for debilitation checks. She'll avoid going below deck when possible, prefers open-air casting, and becomes uncomfortable in confined areas. Imprisonment would be particularly traumatic for her.

Heartbeast Transformation: Instant transformation to gannet form (or back). In gannet form:

  • She's Size -3, fast and maneuverable
  • She can cast spells at -5 penalty (Quiet + Subtle Magic)
  • She's affected by Animal spells, not Corpus/Mentem
  • She can't carry possessions (they don't transform)
  • She's recognized as natural animal by most magic

Visions: Come at storyguide's discretion, usually at quiet moments or places of power. Should be symbolic, confusing, or ambiguous - not clear prophecy. She reports them but doesn't obsess over interpretation. Use them to foreshadow, create atmosphere, or hint at deeper mysteries.

Bras the Capricorn: Intelligent magical companion (grog-level). Can follow complex instructions or disobey on own initiative. Speaks no human language but understands through Animal Ken. Might be present or absent depending on scene needs - he's independent enough to be elsewhere.

Generous Trait: She shares knowledge, resources, and protection freely but with expectation of independence. She'll teach you, equip you, and defend you - then expect you to stand on your own. She becomes frustrated with people who won't use her help to become self-sufficient.

Impatient Trait: Visible restlessness when action is delayed. Pacing, fidgeting, preliminary movements. +3 bonus to rolls when quick action is beneficial; should create problems when patience is required. She'll push for immediate action over careful planning.

Clan Ilfetu Mission: She actively seeks Ilfetu elders across Europe. Meeting them should create story opportunities - they might test her, teach her, request favors, or involve her in House politics. Some are generous mentors; others are difficult teachers. All have knowledge she desperately wants.

Magic Sensitivity: She can sense magic auras and effects more easily than others. This could prove valuable for detecting regio entrances, hidden enchantments, or magical phenomena at sea. However, it provides no detail about what she's sensing - just awareness that magic is present.

Growth Arc: She's developing her maritime magic repertoire. She needs to invent or learn:

  • Weather prediction and control spells
  • Wind guidance for navigation
  • Ship protection wards
  • Lightning control and defense
  • Flight magic for scouting
  • Communication spells for ship-to-ship contact

She has the theoretical foundation (high Arts, good Intelligence, Magic Theory) but needs time in laboratory and practice at sea to build practical capabilities.

Cultural Position: As Norwegian refugee raised in Scotland, she's somewhat stateless - no strong ties to either homeland. House Bjornaer provides her primary identity and community. She's comfortable as outsider, used to not quite fitting anywhere.

multipleCasting.sumary: Int + Finesse + stress die - no of spells VS 9

basicLabTotal.sumary: Int + Magic theory + Aura (+ Technique + Form)

totalPenetration.sumary: Penetration + (Active effects penetration like Arcane connection or Sympathetic Connection)

sigil.label: Wizard's Sigil
sigil.value: A brilliant flash of light high in the atmosphere.

system.description:
The Gift is a special Virtue, because it has no cost. The character suffers all the penalties of The Gift, just as magi do (see page @@), but can be taught Supernatural Abilities without having to take the corresponding Virtues (see page @@ for rules). Most importantly, the character can be taught Hermetic Magic, so all magi must have this Virtue. A character with The Gift, even if he is not a magus, may take Hermetic Virtues and Flaws which relate to intrinsic ability rather than background or training. Characters may take Virtues that grant Supernatural Abilities without taking The Gift, and such characters do not suffer the penalties imposed by The Gift. However, such characters may not learn new Supernatural Abilities in the course of the saga, although they may improve the ones they already have. It is possible for unGifted characters to Initiate new Supernatural Abilities through a Mystery Cult, but even this is harder for them than for the Gifted.

Characters who have The Gift may start play with a single Supernatural Ability, without having to take any other Virtue, but if they wish to learn others they must find opportunities to do so in the course of the saga. They may also take Virtues granting Supernatural Abilities if they wish to have more Abilities at character creation. Note that it is harder for a character with Supernatural Abilities to become a Hermetic magus (see Training Your Apprentice on page @@), so you may not wish to take any such abilities if you plan for the character to become an apprentice. The ability to cast Hermetic magic is the single supernatural ability possessed by Hermetic magi in virtue of The Gift again, they may take more Supernatural Ability Virtues if they wish.

Grogs can never have The Gift, as a character with The Gift is too important to be a grog. As a rule, companions should only have The Gift if they are intended to become magi, or if they are members of one of the other magical traditions described in Ars Magica 5th Edition supplements. Troupes should not allow any other Gifted characters unless they are absolutely sure that they want them.
You are a member of the Order of Hermes. All magi must take this as their Social Status, and only magi may take it.

You have been initiated into the Outer Mystery of the Heartbeast (see page @@), and thus are a member of House Bjornaer. You start with the Ability Heartbeast 1. Note that all Bjornaer magi gain this Virtue for free at character creation.

The character is a member of Clan Ilfetu within House Bjornaer, and has received extra training in the rituals of the House. You receive 50 extra experience points that may be spent on House Bjornaer Lore, Magic Lore (with a specialty in the Great Beasts), and Gothic, the dead language that the House uses for all of its rituals. It is possible that the magus has also been Initiated into the Esoteric Mystery of Divination and Augury (see The Mysteries Revised Edition, page 58); if so, that Virtue must be purchased with the normal allowance of ten points of Virtues and Flaws. Clan Ilfetu teaches the Divination method of haemagmomancy, which is divination by observing the blood splatters of wounded animals; this gives the following bonuses to Divination: +5 family, +3 Corpus. See Houses of Hermes: Mystery Cults, page 12, for more details on House Bjornaer and the role of Clan Ilfetu.

You can cast spells using only a soft voice at no penalty, and at only a –5 penalty if you do not speak at all. You gain no benefits from using your voice normally but gain the normal benefit for using a booming voice. The range of Voice Range spells is determined normally, based on how loud your voice is.

 Use "Silent Magic" to not speak at all without penalty

Your memory has been developed to remember magical rather than mundane things. You need not keep laboratory texts (see page @@) of your creations to get the benefit of a Lab Text when reproducing them. If you have created an effect by following another magus’s Lab Text once, you may get the same benefit in future without needing to have the text available.
You can communicate with animals as if they were human beings. Choosing this Virtue confers the Ability Animal Ken 1 (page @@).

You ha ve a deep understanding of a type of terrain, feeling more natural and at home there than anywhere else. Examples include Ways of the Forest, Ways of the Mountain, Ways of the Steppe or Ways of the Town. You get a +3 bonus to all rolls, including combat and Casting Scores, that directly involve that area and its inhabitants; mundane, magical, or faerie. In addition, you roll one fewer botch die than normal (which may mean you roll no botch dice) in rolls that pertain to your area of understanding. You are not normally molested by animals of the terrain, and though particularly vicious creatures may still attack you, at least they’ll accord you proper respect. (For Ways of the Town, this still applies to town animals, of which there are plenty in the medieval world, rather than people. The +3 bonus does apply to people.) You may choose this Virtue multiple times, for different types of terrain.

You have an additional three points to spend on buying Characteristics, but you are still limited to a maximum score of +3 in any single Characteristic unless you take the Virtue Great Characteristic. You may take this Virtue multiple times.

This Virtue is associated with one of five classes of beings: animals, divine beings, faeries, demons, or magical creatures. These last four include characters associated with the Divine, Faerie, Infernal, or Magic realm, respectively, through Supernatural Virtues or Flaws, as well as beings with Might. 

The character’s Gift does not bother beings of this sort, although it still has the normal effects on others. For example, Inoffensive to Animals makes it easier for her to get along with mundane beasts. Animals who react positively to The Gift still react positively to her, since she does have The Gift, but those that do not are not disturbed by her presence. 

You may not take this Virtue more than once; characters who are Inoffensive to more than one type of being should take Gentle Gift instead. UnGifted characters may take this Virtue only if they have the Flaw Magical Air.

Note that Inoffensive to Mundane Humans is not available as a Minor Virtue; take Gentle Gift instead.
You can see farther and more clearly than most. You get a +3 bonus to all rolls involving sight, not including attacks with missile weapons.

You respond to surprises almost instantly. In fact, your reflexes are sometimes so fast that you don

’t have a chance to think about how you are going to respond. Whenever you are surprised or startled, roll a stress die + Quickness. If you get a 3 or better, you respond reflexively. You must tell the storyguide on each occasion what one type of action (attacking, blocking, running, etc.) you would like to respond with. If attacking in response, you gain +9 to your Initiative Total. The storyguide is the final arbiter of what happens (though it is always in the best interests of your immediate self-preservation). You only react to threats that you are not fully aware of, so you don’t get a bonus against an assassin you watch sneak up on you. Note that you do not get a choice about whether to react. You could just as easily skewer a friend sneaking up in fun as you would an assassin about to strike. Also note that you must perceive an action to react to it — you can still be easily killed in your sleep. This Virtue gives you no special powers of perception.

You are unable to study magic from books or vis alone. You must study in the presence of the appropriate Art. For example, you need to sit next to a brook or pond to study Aquam, or a large fire to study Ignem. Growing things are good for Creo, decaying ones good for Perdo. As your knowledge grows, you need to work with larger and larger quantities. See the Study Bonus Virtue on page @@ for a list of examples.

You may take both Study Bonus and Study Requirement.
You are slapdash in your use of magical power, and must roll two more botch dice than normal when casting spells. Note that if you would normally roll no botch dice, you still roll none.

Almost all totals (including Casting Totals and Lab Totals, but excluding Magic Resistance) to which a particular Form is added are halved. Advancement Totals are not halved. Experience points required are based on the actual value of the Technique, before halving.

You often see images related to emotionally or magically laden events. A vision might be of the past, a possible future, or a distant occurrence, and is often symbolic or confusing. Visions usually come to you at quiet times or in places connected with a powerful emotional or magical event, such as the site of a patricide or diabolic sacrifice. Your visions may warn you of dangers to come, or get you involved in matters you would otherwise have avoided.

The visions come purely at the storyguide's discretion, and reveal only what he wants to reveal.

The character is accompanied by an intelligent magical being, one that is smart enough to follow orders or disobey them on its own initiative, which the character must regularly look after. This might be an animal, a spirit, a human-like creature, or even an animate object. Realms of Power: Magic contains extensive rules for designing such characters, but they can also be made following the guidelines and examples in Chapter 13 of this book. A grog-level character might be essentially an extension of the character’s will, while a companion-level character might follow instructions but often do what it thinks is best, and a magus-level character might be condescending or wild, and rarely act except on its own initiative. 

A particularly common example is a magical animal that’s smart enough to follow your orders or to disobey them on its own initiative. The smaller and more innocuous the creature, the more intelligent it is. A ferret or crow is as intelligent as a human, a wolf is very cunning, and an animal the size of a horse is simply more intelligent than normal. The creature has a Magic Might score of 10 – Size.

You have some goal which you are determined to bring about. This has to be the sort of big goal that could shape an entire life, but if you do accomplish it you will immediately turn to a new project.

You are of a lighter-than-average build. Your Size is reduced to –1. This means that the severity of wounds you take increases in four point increments, rather than five point. (See page @@). You cannot take this Flaw and Giant Blood (page @@), Large (page @@), or Dwarf (page @@).
You may cast spells without using gestures at no penalty. You gain no benefits from using normal gestures but gain the normal benefit for exaggerated gestures.

The character is unusually sensitive to an uncommon environmental feature, such as bad air, salt water, or extreme temperatures. Whenever the character is exposed to these conditions, he has a –3 penalty to his Stamina for any debilitation checks (page @@).

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Noticing things, be they things you are looking for or things that you are not expecting. It is also used to see how alert you are in circumstances that require watchfulness.

Specialties: bodyguarding, keeping watch, alertness, searching. (General)

General physical prowess. It includes moving smoothly, confidently, and with grace. Use of Athletics improves most large-muscle coordination.

Specialties: acrobatics, contortions, grace, jumping, running. (General)

Focusing your mental faculties on one task, particularly for extended periods of time. If you are attempting a feat that demands your extra attention, or if you have just failed an action and are trying again, the storyguide can call for a Concentration roll before you can make the attempt. This Ability is especially important for magi because it helps them maintain concentration on spells despite distractions.

Specialties: spell concentration, reading, lab work. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

0

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

The ability to propel yourself through water quickly and efficiently.

Specialties: long distances, diving, underwater maneuvering. (General)

The seven artes liberales, or liberal arts, are the basis of medieval higher education. Everyone learns them at the universities before passing on to study other subjects. It would be very rare for a character to have a score in any other Academic Knowledge without a score of at least one in this.

The artes liberales are divided into two groups: the Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). The emphasis at this period is on the Trivium, but both are taught. (Note that Artes Liberales as a whole is one Skill, not seven separate ones.)

Grammar concerns theoretical questions of the structure of languages, rather than the ability to use a specific language (that is covered by Speak skills). The authorities are Priscian and Donatus. Logic is the study of the syllogism, and other forms of reasoning discussed by Aristotle. It is the most important of the arts at this period. Aristotle is the authority, in his logica vetus and logica nova. Rhetoric is the study of the theory of rhetorical forms: knowledge of the sort of tricks that an orator can use, rather than the ability to use them. The authority is Cicero, especially his De Inventione.

Arithmetic is the ability to add and subtract, multiply and divide. The authority is Boethius, De arithmetica. Geometry is concerned with study of plane and solid figures, and the authority is Euclid, in his Elementa. It also deals with the study of light, and Euclid's Optica is the authority here. Astronomy is concerned with predicting the positions of the stars and planets, and the authority is Ptolemy, in the Libri Almagesti. Music is purely theoretical, the study of proportions and musical theory, not the ability to sing or play an instrument. The authority is Boethius, in De Musica.

Artes Liberales also covers the ability to read and write. For every point in the Ability, the character can read and write one writing system, providing that he knows a language which normally uses that system: the Latin alphabet, the Greek alphabet, the Arabic alphabet, and so on.

Specialties: grammar, logic, rhetoric, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

Knowledge of magical creatures, areas, and traditions.

Specialties: creatures, magical traditions, regiones. (Arcane)

Knowledge of what magic is and how it works, used primarily in the laboratory. Magic Theory deals with the technical details of Hermetic magic; Magic Lore covers knowledge of magical things in general. Anyone can learn Magic Theory, if they have access to a teacher or book (normally represented by a Virtue), but it is little use to those without The Gift.

Specialties: inventing spells, enchanting items, a single Art. (Arcane)

Protection from magic. This is a special ritual (not a ritual spell) that takes about two minutes to perform. It lets you add 5 times your Parma Magica score to your Magic Resistance until the next sunrise or sunset, whichever comes first. You may suppress your Parma Magica temporarily by concentrating; this is equivalent to sustaining a Concentration duration spell. (See page @@). Parma Magica does not require concentration while it is active.

You may also protect one other person for each point in Parma Magica, with their consent. You must touch each person to start the protection, and it lasts as long as at least one character can see the other. The magus may cancel the protection at will, at any distance. While a magus is protecting others, his effective Parma Magica score is reduced by 3 points, both for himself and for the other people he is protecting. If the magus has a Parma Magica score of 3 or lower, his Parma Magica provides each character, including himself, with a Magic Resistance of 0. In the magus's case, this is added to his Form resistance, and a magus protected by another magus's Parma Magica may also add his Form resistance.

Any character protected by a Parma Magica, whether their own or someone else’s, is unaffected by the social penalties of other people’s Gifts. If a protected character is Gifted, their Gift still imposes social penalties on others, unless those others are also protected by a Parma Magica, or are unaffected by The Gift for some other reason.

Parma Magica can only be learned by Gifted characters, although they learn it as a normal Arcane Ability, not a Supernatural Ability. It is only known by Hermetic magi, as the Order enforces the 'Join or Die' choice rigorously on anyone who knows it, as well as declaring a Wizard's March on the magus who taught it. Parma Magica is the last thing an apprentice learns, being taught the final key to the Ability after he swears the Oath.

Specialties: protection from any specific Form. (Arcane)

You can communicate with animals as if they were human beings. Treat your score in Animal Ken as your score in a language that the animal speaks fluently in order to determine how well you can communicate, and you can use Animal Handling as a substitute for any social abilities affecting humans. Beyond this, this Ability has no effect on the attitude of animals to you, or you to animals. Other people cannot understand your communication with the animals.

Specialties: a particular type of animal, a particular type of communication. (Supernatural)

Bjornaer magi can take the form of a single mundane creature. This is usually an animal or bird, but it can be a plant. The heartbeast is always a living thing, and usually a noble creature. No known magus has had an earthworm heartbeast, for example. A maga’s heartbeast displays a central aspect of her nature, so that her personality, even in human form, will resemble that of the creature. Initiates of the Bjornaer mystery gain the Ability Heartbeast. This Ability cannot be gained by any character who has not been initiated into the mystery. For initiates of the Outer Mystery, the Heartbeast Ability is only used when something tries to stop the magus changing forms. In that case, a roll of Stamina + Heartbeast against an Ease Factor set by the storyguide allows the character to change anyway.

A Bjornaer maga can only change into one kind of animal, and that kind cannot be altered by Hermetic magic. The transformed Bjornaer can be changed, by Muto Animal magic, for example, but the kind of animal that her heartbeast is cannot be changed. A Bjornaer maga really is her heartbeast, and so remaining in that form does not count as being under an active mystical effect (see “Warping,“ page 167). Of course, she is really human as well, so remaining human does not cause warping either. Hermetic magic is unable to tell whether an animal is actually a Bjornaer, and the same applies to most other kinds of magic. (This assumes that the maga is trying to hide her nature, of course.) As a result, a Bjornaer in animal form is affected by Animal spells, not Corpus or Mentem spells. Spells already in effect before the transformation remain in effect, however.

Bjornaer magi can cast spells while in the form of their heartbeast, but they cannot speak or make the appropriate gestures, and thus normally take a –15 penalty.

The transformation takes only a moment, and affects only the maga. None of her possessions are transformed. A Bjornaer who has been physically changed by magic may try to assume her normal form or her heartbeast form. She must roll Stamina + Heartbeast against an Ease Factor set by the storyguide. As a rule of thumb, 3 + the magnitude of the transforming magic is a reasonable number. If she does resume one of her natural forms, the other magic is dispelled.

Bjornaer magi do not treat physical transformations as major magical effects for the purposes of gaining Warping Points. However, a magical transformation which is maintained over time will grant one Warping Point per year, as it is still a continuing mystical effect.

Finally, Bjornaer magi cannot bind familiars. The reasons for this are debated, but the fact is uncontroversial.

items.name: Ward Against Faeries of the Waters

system.description:
No water faerie whose Faerie Might is equal to or less than the level of the spell can affect those targeted by the spell. Faeries cannot directly or indirectly break the magic circle, nor can they use ranged attacks or magic to affect those within it.

Seen from certain angles at night, the ring appears as a light blue dome.

Creates a thunderclap; anyone directly underneath must make a Stamina stress roll of 9+ or be deafened. If deafened, the target gets another Stamina simple roll each minute and recovers with a roll of 9+. If the first roll botches, the victim is rendered deaf for a month.

Creates a strong undertow that pulls any object smaller than a rowboat 25 fathoms (150 feet) into the depths. Each of those caught in the undercurrent must make a Strength + Swim stress roll against an Ease Factor of 9 to avoid being dragged down. A roll is made each round that the spell is maintained.

You can make an animal perform any act it is capable of. You can only issue new commands while touching the animal, but the animal will carry them out without requiring constant contact. Further, the animal will allow you to touch it throughout the duration of the spell. It is difficult to maintain control of particularly stubborn or fierce animals, such as mules or boars — creatures with appropriate Personality Traits may roll against an Ease Factor of 12 each round.

When Chavin of Tytalus casts this spell, the creature acts surly and unfriendly while going about its tasks, even when Chavin doesn't specifically tell it to act that way.
This spell heals any animal disease, instantly easing the beast’s suffering. St Francis was a legendary healer of animals. This spell works on animals Size +2 or smaller.

items.name: The Voice of the Bjornaer Magus

system.description: This spell allows the caster to speak with a human voice when shapechanged into the form of an animal. The caster must be in animal form to cast this spell because it targets the vocal capacity of that form, and must be able to cast this spell with no words or gestures (usually a -15 penalty), but while under its influence, may use his own voice without penalty to spellcasting.
